macro_segments.title: The Indirect Touch with No Intent Case: Tosafos's Reinterpretation of the Palm Branch Story

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the daf and overview of touch categories that make wine forbidden
The palm branch story: A non-Jew coming from a tree touching wine unintentionally with the tip of a branch
Tosafos's analysis: The combination of indirect touch plus no intent to touch makes wine completely permissible, even for drinking
Tosafos's reading of the text: The story must be about direct touch, not indirect touch, because indirect plus no intent would be universally permitted

macro_segments.title: The Infant and Contemporary Christians: Extension of Shmuel's Leniency to Modern Non-Jews

micro_segments.title: The dispute between Rav and Shmuel about whether an infant makes wine forbidden: Rav says yes to drinking, Shmuel says no
Tosafos's argument: Modern Christians are like infants because they don't know about idolatry in the context of wine libations
The radical conclusion Tosafos could have drawn: If Christians are like infants and we rule like Shmuel, even drinking their wine should be permitted
Tosafos's actual ruling: Don't drink but you can get benefit from Christian wine, splitting between the idolatry concern and the intermarriage concern
The logic behind the ruling: Idolatry concern removed because Christians don't use wine for libations; intermarriage concern remains, so don't drink but benefit is okay

macro_segments.title: The Angry Non-Jew Incident: Direct Touch with Intent to Touch Wine but No Intent for Libations

micro_segments.title: The story: A non-Jew comes to a Jewish store, gets angry about not selling wine, sticks his hand in and moves it around
Rava permits selling the wine; Rav Huna bar Chinena and Rav Huna son of Rav Nachman forbid it
Rava asks his teacher Rav Huna to clarify: Were you saying direct touch without intent for libation is permissible?
Rav Huna's clarification: I meant if the forbidden wine gets mixed with other wine, subtract its value and sell the rest
Rava goes to Pumbedisa where Abaye challenges him with stories showing direct touch makes wine forbidden even without intent for libation
The inspector story: A government wine inspector tastes wine to check quality and puts it back, which Abaye says proves wine is forbidden in benefit
The second case: A non-Jew puts his hand in thinking it's oil but it's wine, and the conclusion is that wine is forbidden in benefit

macro_segments.title: The Pouring Incident and Establishing the Four-Category Matrix

micro_segments.title: The story: Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yosi ben Nehorai ask a man to pour wine; it turns out he was a non-Jew
One opinion says forbidden in benefit; the other says permissible even for drinking; Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi says each has merit
The opinion forbidding it assumes the non-Jew knew it was wine and therefore touched it with awareness of a problem
The opinion permitting it assumes the non-Jew thought it was beer, not wine, because he knew rabbis don't ask non-Jews to touch wine
The Gemara resolves: The non-Jew touched it indirectly through a scoop or pitcher, not directly, and didn't think it was wine, so it's completely permissible
Principle established: When there's no intent to touch (at all), the Sages did not make a decree forbidding the wine

macro_segments.title: The Four-Category Matrix: Mapping Intent, Directness, and Forbidden Status

micro_segments.title: Category 1: Direct touch with intent to touch wine = forbidden in benefit (assur behana'ah)
Category 2: Direct touch without intent to touch wine = forbidden only for drinking (assur beshtiyah)
Category 3: Indirect touch without intent to touch wine = completely permissible (mutar)
Category 4: Indirect touch with intent to touch wine = forbidden for drinking, possibly permissible for benefit
Summary principle: Two good factors (indirect + no intent) = permissible; one good one bad = forbidden for drinking; two bad factors (direct + intent) = forbidden in benefit

macro_segments.title: Water Mixed into Wine and Rabbinic Safeguards

micro_segments.title: Question: If a non-Jew only adds water to wine without touching the wine itself, is it forbidden?
Answer: Even if he doesn't touch the wine, it's forbidden as a rabbinic safeguard (lech lech to the nazir analogy)
Story of Rav Yirmeyah in Safta: Non-Jews mixed water into wine and Jews were drinking; he forbade it as a safeguard
General principle restated: Non-Jews mixing water into wine is forbidden, though technically perhaps permissible

macro_segments.title: The Botzrah Digression: Terumot and Ma'asrot Outside the Land of Israel

micro_segments.title: Story of Resh Lakish in Botzrah: He forbade eating untithed fruit and wine that non-Jews had bowed to
Rav Yochanan corrects him: Botzrah is outside the Land of Israel where terumot and ma'asrot don't apply
Discussion of whether terumot and ma'asrot apply outside the Land of Israel in immediate adjoining areas versus distant lands
Community-owned water cannot be forbidden by worship because it doesn't belong to individuals

macro_segments.title: Or Zarua's Practical Ruling: Direct Touch Permissible But with Important Caveat

micro_segments.title: Or Zarua rules: In modern times when a non-Jew moves his hand in Jewish wine, you can sell it to non-Jews
Reasoning: Modern non-Jews are not skilled in the technicalities and cannot make wine forbidden in benefit; drinking is forbidden
Critical caveat: This leniency applies only if the non-Jew didn't intend to anger the Jew and ruin him by making wine forbidden
If the non-Jew explicitly intends to make wine forbidden to harm the Jew, even direct touch doesn't forbid it in benefit or for drinking
Historical example: A pious elder drank directly from wine a non-Jew had touched to demonstrate to him that he couldn't make it forbidden

topical_tags.term: כוונת ניסוך (kavanat nisech / intent for libation)
Rav vs. Shmuel (Infant makes wine forbidden dispute)
Tosafos on Christians as like infants
Rava vs. Rav Huna (Direct touch dispute)
